VARIANT QUALITY CHECKS AND FILTERING REPORT

Author

Phelelani Mpangase

Published

2025 September 28

This document outlines the filtering strategy employed during the secondary analysis of genetic variants, with a focus on prioritization criteria informed by functional annotation. The filtering process is guided by annotations generated using the Ensembl Variant Effect Predictor (VEP) - which provides comprehensive insights across multiple biological dimensions - as well as the quality of the dataset. These criteria collectively support the systematic refinement of candidate variants, enabling focused down-stream analysis aligned with the study’s objectives.

Import Necessary Libraries

Define Resources And Settings

This report was generated on the 2025/09/28 at 20:34:47

Set Input And Output Folders

1 Raw Data Cleanup

Before we perform any filtering, we will first look at the dataset and general quality metrics that will be used for the filtering.

1.1 Raw Data Quality Checks

This dataset contains 3978351 variants and 20 samples. For quality metrics, we will look at:

  • Genotype Quality (GQ)
  • Read Depth (DP)
  • Call Rate (CR)
  • CR vs GQ
  • CR vs DP
  • Allele Frequency (AF)

The GQ represents the Phred-scaled confidence that the genotype assignment (GT) is correct, derived from the “Phred-scaled likelihood of the genotype” (PLs). Specifically, the GQ is the difference between the PL of the second most likely genotype, and the PL of the most likely genotype. Basically the GQ gives you the difference between the likelihoods of the two most likely genotypes. If it is low, you can tell there is not much confidence in the genotype, i.e. there was not enough evidence to confidently choose one genotype over another.

Important

We set the lowest GQ cutoff at 10, i.e., we have no confidence in any genotype with a GQ of less than 10.

DP is the filtered depth at the sample level. This gives you the number of filtered reads that support each of the reported alleles. In other words, DP represents the total number of sequence reads that align to and overlap a specific genomic position, providing insight into the coverage and confidence of variant calls at that site. Higher DP values increase confidence that a called variant is a true biological alteration rather than a sequencing error, especially when correlated with the Allelic Depth (AD) field, which specifies reads supporting the alternate versus the reference allele.

Important

We set the lowest DP cutoff at 10, i.e., we have no confidence in any genotype with an AD of less than 10.

The CR for a given sample the proportion of genotypes that were successfully recorded (not missing) with in a study cohort. CR is calculated by dividing the number of non-missing genotype calls for a sample by the total number of genotypes the sample was intended to have. A high sample call rate indicates good quality data from that individual, while a low call rate may signal poor sample quality, insufficient DNA, or genotyping issues. Individuals with low sample call rates are often removed from analysis because the missing data could negatively impact the power of statistical analyses or introduce bias.

Important

We lowest CR to 0.9 (90%), i.e., we retain samples for which there is less than 10% missing calls.

Important

We set the lowest DP cutoff at 10, i.e., we have no confidence in any genotype with an AD of less than 10.

Important

We set the lowest DP cutoff at 10, i.e., we have no confidence in any genotype with an AD of less than 10.

We will also look at the distribution of in the dataset, as this is very useful in the process of filtering for rare disease-causeing alleles in populations.

Important

We set the highest AF cutoff at 0.05 (5%), i.e., we have no confidence in any genotype with an AF above 0.05 (5%)

1.2 Check & Exclude Multi-Allelic Positions

Multi-allelic positions are positions in a genome where three or more distinct variants (alleles) are present, rather than just two as seen in bi-allelic sites. Multi-allellic sites are such a pain to work with - we prefere to split these sites to bi-allelic before annotation (if we did joint calling and annotation for you, no need to worry). This is just a sanity-check step to make sure that we aren’t working with any multi-allelic sites.

1.3 Remove NA’s

Here… Some variants have missing or no genotype assigned (GT) to a sample, or they have missing allele depth (AD). We want to exclude these from the analysis.

Fix VEP Annotations

Save to Disk

Load from Disk

2 Variant Filtering

2.1 Calculated Consequences

The IMPACT annotation from the Variant Effect Predictor (VEP) classifies variants into four categories based on their predicted effect on gene function: HIGH, MODERATE, LOW, and MODIFIER. In this filtering step, variants were selected based on their assigned impact level, retaining only those that matched the predefined criteria of interest. This approach helps prioritize variants with greater potential for functional relevance, depending on the biological or clinical context of the analysis.

In addition to IMPACT, we also have “Consequence” annotations (Figure 13). Consequence annotations describe the specific effect a variant may have on the transcript or protein e.g. whether it results in a missense change, a stop-gained mutation, or a splice-site disruption. Only variants with consequences of interest to the disease context were retained. For reference, the full list of consequence terms annotated by VEP can be accessed here. Filtering typically involves including high-impact changes such as missense, nonsense, frameshift, and splice-site variants, while excluding synonymous or non-coding variants unless otherwise justified. By focusing on these functionally significant consequences, the filtering strategy enhances the likelihood of identifying variants with potential pathogenic roles.

Ensembl Variation

Before we do any filtering, let us look at how many variants fall within the IMPACT and Consequence categores (Figure 14)

We are not interested in “less severe” Consequences, falling in the “LOW” and “MODIFIER” IMPACT!.

Important

There are a number of consequence terms defined by the Sequence Ontology (SO). We are particularly not interested in the less severe SO terms. These SO terms are excluded from the analysis, and include:

  • incomplete_terminal_codon_variant
  • intergenic_variant
  • intron_variant
  • non_coding_transcript_exon_variant
  • non_coding_transcript_variant
  • splice_donor_5th_base_variant
  • splice_donor_region_variant
  • splice_polypyrimidine_tract_variantsplice_region_variant
  • start_retained_variant
  • stop_retained_variant
  • synonymous_variant

Now, we apply our first IMPACT (LOW) filter:

Filtering 1.17% rows out of downstream analysis.

2.1.2 MODIFIER IMPACT Filter

Then we apply the second IMPACT (MODIFIER) filter:

Filtering 84.65% rows out of downstream analysis.

2.2 African Allele Frequency Filter

Allele frequency refers to the proportion of a specific allele within a population, calculated by considering the total number of alleles contributed by individuals with different genotypes at a particular locus. There are database dedicated to providing population-specific allele frequencies, including a African populations. These databases include gnomAD and 1000 Genomes Projec. VEP uses these databases to annotate variants with African-specific allele frequencies.

In this filtering step, variants were evaluated based on their allele frequency within this population group, using thresholds aligned with the study’s focus on rare or potentially pathogenic variants. We use an allele frequency threshold of 0.05 (5%) to prioritize rare variants.

Filtering 2.20% rows out of downstream analysis.

2.3 Allele Frequency Filter

Same as above, but looking at allele frequencies calculated within the cohort.

Important

If the cohort size is small, allele frequencies will not yield any positive results. Have a look at the allele frequency distribution plot in Section 1.4 to see if you have any variants with an AF below 0.05, otherwise we might have to skip, or adjust the threshold. We can also look at what the smaallest possible nonzero allele frequency is based on the sample size, as well as what the smallest allele frequency in the dataset is.

Minimum possible nonzero AF for this "ovarian_cancer" project: 0.0250
Minimum nonzero AF in this "ovarian_cancer" project: 0.0263
Filtering 0.02% rows out of downstream analysis.

  • Homozygous Reference
  • Heterozygous

Filtering 67.87% entries out of downstream analysis.

2.5 Homozygous Reference Filter

In this filtering step, we remove homozygous reference (GT: 0/0) variants from the analysis. These variants indicate that the alternate allele matches the reference genome - thus, no observed variance. Removing homozygous reference calls helps focus the analysis on sites with observed variation.

Filtering 62.66% entries out of downstream analysis.

2.6 Prediction Score(s) Filter

Computational damage scores are predictive metrics that estimate the potential functional impact of genetic variants using in silico computational methods. In this filtering step, variants are evaluated based on their assigned damage scores from tools such as CADD, REVEL, PolyPhen-2, or SIFT. Variants that do not meet the predefined thresholds (), based on each tool, are excluded the analysis. This approach helps prioritize variants with greater potential to disrupt protein function or contribute to disease, especially when experimental validation is limited. Integrating multiple damage scores enhances confidence in variant selection and supports more informed downstream interpretation.

Filtering 3.02% rows out of downstream analysis.

2.7 Empty Variants/Sample Filter

This is just a “sanity” step. During variant filtering, some samples can end up with no variants, and some variants can end up with no samples. This is to make sure that all samples with no variants, and all variants with no samples are removed from the dataset.

2.8 Candidate Gene(s) Filter

In this [final] filtering step, variant filtering is performed to include only those variants located within a predefined set of genes known to be associated with the disease under investigation. Each gene was mapped to its genomic coordinates, and these coordinates were used to extract variants falling within the corresponding regions of interest. The gene list is curated by investigator based on prior literature, known disease mechanisms, and clinical relevance.

Candidate gene list provided with a total of 97 genes!
Filtering 99.32% rows out of downstream analysis.
